Ayah 1–2 · linked video: 01. Al-Munafiqun (Ayah 1-2)

Surah Al-Munafiqun: Commentary on Ayahs 1-2

Ayah 1

إِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ قَالُوا۟ نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

Surah Al-Munafiqun is dedicated to the most comprehensive study of the hypocrites found in the Quran. While longer passages about hypocrisy exist in Surah Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, Al-Tawbah and Al-Ahzab, this surah serves as an essential overview that brings everything together in a remarkably concise way. This is the first place a student should study hypocrisy before exploring the topic elsewhere in the Quran, as it establishes the foundational understanding of this spiritual disease.

The surah continues the conversation from Surah Al-Jumu’ah, drawing a powerful parallel between the hypocrites of Madinah and the failings among Bani Isra’il. Both groups profess belief without acting upon it, exhibiting a dangerous disconnect between what they say and what they do. This connection is reinforced throughout the Quran—in Surah As-Saff, Allah ﷻ asks, “Why do you say what you don’t do?” and immediately follows with mentions of the followers of Musa (AS) and Isa (AS), both from Bani Isra’il, highlighting how the criminal element among previous nations exhibited similar hypocritical behavior.

The Inevitable Approach of the Hypocrites

The ayah opens with إِذَا جَآءَكَ —“when they come to you.” The particle إِذَا is significant here. Unlike إِن , which suggests “if” and carries uncertainty, إِذَا indicates certainty and inevitability. This isn’t “if they come to you” but “when they come to you”—it’s going to happen. The hypocrites have a particular agenda, and part of that agenda is to create the appearance of being close to the Prophet ﷺ, of having privileged access to him.

We saw this pattern in Surah Al-Mujadilah and Surah Al-Hashr, where hypocrites wanted to speak with the Prophet ﷺ, and Allah ﷻ revealed specific etiquette just to prevent them from making it look like they had an intimate personal relationship with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. What they sought was essentially a photo opportunity—a chance to stand next to him and be seen in his presence.

The Over-Emphatic Declaration

When the hypocrites come to the Prophet ﷺ, they say: نَشْهَدُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ—“We bear witness that you are truly the Messenger of Allah.” This statement is loaded with multiple layers of emphasis that expose their deception:

First, they use a verbal sentence (جملة فعلية) beginning with نَشْهَدُ —“we testify.” Second, they add إِنَّ , a particle of emphasis. Third, they include the lam of emphasis (لَ) in لَرَسُولُ . This isn’t simply saying “you are the Messenger of Allah”—it’s an exaggerated, over-the-top declaration designed to impress.

Here’s a crucial linguistic point: there’s a fundamental difference between “I testify that Islam is the truth” and simply stating “Islam is the truth.” When you say “I testify,” you’re making a claim about yourself and your belief—a claim that could be true or false, since you might be lying about what you actually believe. But when you simply state “Islam is the truth” without reference to yourself, that statement stands independent of your personal belief. It’s objectively true whether you believe it or not.

The hypocrites come to the Prophet ﷺ with this overcompensating testimony, going out of their way to express excessive faith in him. This over-emphasis itself becomes a red flag. Those who genuinely believe in the Prophet ﷺ don’t approach him with such obvious declarations. They don’t say, “You really are the Messenger! Seriously, you’re SO the Messenger!” When someone engages in this kind of over-emphatic stating of the obvious, it signals either sarcasm or an attempt to convince someone of something false—like a dishonest salesperson who talks too much, making excessive promises to close a deal.

Allah’s Response and the Exposed Lie

Allah ﷻ responds with remarkable clarity: وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِنَّكَ لَرَسُولُهُۥ—“And Allah knows that you are indeed His Messenger.” The irony is palpable. Thank you, Captain Obvious. Allah ﷻ certainly doesn’t need their testimony to confirm what He already knows.

Then comes the devastating conclusion: وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ—“And Allah testifies that the hypocrites are truly liars.” But wait—what exactly are they lying about? The statement “you are the Messenger of Allah” is actually true. The Prophet ﷺ is indeed the Messenger of Allah ﷻ, so that part isn’t false. The lie is in their claim “we testify” (نَشْهَدُ). They testify with their mouths but not with their hearts. That’s where the deception lies, and that’s what Allah ﷻ calls out.

Notice the eloquence of the Quran here. If the ayah had simply said, “When the hypocrites come to you, they say you are the Messenger of Allah,” and Allah ﷻ called them liars, it would create confusion—because that statement is objectively true. But by including نَشْهَدُ (we testify), Allah ﷻ exposes the specific lie: they’re not really testifying or declaring anything genuine. It’s entirely fake.

A Personal Warning About Displaying Faith

The major lesson in this ayah extends beyond identifying others—it’s a mirror for self-examination. The hypocrite goes out of his way to profess iman, and this becomes one of the recognizable signs of hypocrisy. This isn’t about judging others; it’s about watching yourself.

You don’t need to broadcast your worship to others. If you prayed extra long in qiyam, if your neck hurts from reciting multiple surahs, if you finished the entire 29th juz with proper tajweed—Allah ﷻ knows. You don’t need to email anyone about it, send out a tweet, or casually mention it in conversation. You don’t have to tell people about your iman or announce your humility. In fact, doing so is a dead giveaway that something is wrong.

Those personal, intimate moments you have with Allah ﷻ are between you and Him. They’re not to be shared with anybody else. They’re sacred and private. If you find yourself wanting to publicize your worship, to let people know about your spiritual achievements, then it’s obviously fake. Keep it to yourself. Let your actions speak quietly, and let your relationship with Allah ﷻ remain in the realm of sincerity.

Ayah 2

ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

The Shield of False Oaths

ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً—“They took their oaths as a shield.” The word جُنَّة means shield or cover—anything that protects or conceals you. The hypocrites’ testimony of faith—their declaration of لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّٰهُ —becomes a shield that protects them from scrutiny.

When someone testifies that they are Muslim, they become safe from other Muslims. Muslims cannot attack them or criticize them because Muslims are commanded to be humble and merciful toward one another. Allah ﷻ tells us not to say to anyone who offers the greeting of peace, “You are not a believer” (4:94). As long as someone says salam, as long as they declare the shahada, they are considered a believer and must not be judged. The hypocrites exploit this protection—they want to maintain their cover, to be safe while doing whatever they intend to disrupt the Prophet’s work ﷺ. They cannot afford to be scrutinized by the Muslim community, so their shahada becomes just a fake shield. The testimony itself is fraudulent.

The Stages of Hypocrisy

This leads us to a critical understanding: hypocrisy develops in stages. It doesn’t appear overnight as full-blown deception. It begins with weak faith (ضعف الإيمان), which results from weak actions—staying away from believers, not seeking enough spiritual nourishment and purification. One of the mechanisms designed to heal us from hypocrisy was mentioned in the previous surah, Surah Al-Jumu’ah. Attending the Friday prayer, hearing the reminder, gathering with the community—these are meant to clean us up spiritually, to prevent the slide into hypocrisy.

The early onset of hypocrisy manifests through recognizable signs. The Prophet ﷺ mentioned four major signs that we should watch out for in ourselves. Scholars have listed up to 28 characteristics, but these four are fundamental:

  1. Breaking promises (إذا وعد أخلف): When making a promise becomes easy to break, when commitments mean nothing.

  2. Lying (إذا حدث كذب): When speaking falsely becomes no big deal—a little exaggeration here, a small misquote there, just to impress people or enhance a story. “Who’s going to catch that anyway?”

  3. Violating trust (إذا اؤتمن خان): When someone cannot be trusted with responsibilities. They say “trust me, I got this,” but they don’t. Breaking trust becomes a pattern.

  4. Becoming aggressive when corrected (إذا خاصم فجر): When criticized, critiqued or corrected, the person explodes. They cannot handle criticism. A personality disorder emerges when you offer them sincere advice or correction—“Who are you to tell me I’m wrong? Know your place!” They become intensely offended by anyone offering critical counsel.

These are personality flaws that indicate varying degrees of hypocrisy. You could have a bit of each, or just one without the others, meaning you have some degree of hypocrisy. We all might carry traces of these traits. But when it reaches a certain intensity, hypocrisy divides into two categories: conscious and subconscious.

Conscious hypocrisy (النفاق الأكبر) is the spy—someone who claims to be Muslim but knows deep down they don’t believe any of it. They have some political agenda, covert operations, gains to make by appearing to be with the Muslims because Muslims are the dominant party. But they have no interest in Islam whatsoever. It’s entirely a facade, and they themselves know it. This is espionage, pure and simple.

Subconscious or unconscious hypocrisy (النفاق الأصغر) is more dangerous in some ways. A person exhibits the behavioral signs that the Prophet ﷺ warned about without even realizing they suffer from hypocrisy. Surah At-Tawbah describes hypocrites who “do not come to prayer except lazily” (9:54). The previous surah, Al-Jumu’ah, told us to rush to Friday prayer—the hypocrites do the opposite. They come dragging their feet, reluctant. And when they give in charity, “they do not spend except unwillingly” (9:54)—they regret it afterward, thinking “I can’t believe I wrote such a big check.”

Someone can exhibit all these signs—laziness in prayer, reluctance to spend, distance from the community—without ever recognizing that they are suffering from hypocrisy.

The Severity of Hypocrisy in the Quran

Now, I’ve made a distinction between conscious and subconscious hypocrisy, but the Quran doesn’t make that distinction. As far as the Quran is concerned, it doesn’t matter. Whether you’re a fake Muslim intentionally and ideologically, or you’re a fake Muslim behaviorally without full awareness—you are still a hypocrite (منافق).

What’s striking is that hypocrisy is discussed in the Quran in more severe terms than even shirk. We were raised learning that shirk is the unforgivable sin—“Indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners be associated with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills” (4:48). Yet in the case of hypocrisy, the conversation becomes even more intense.

The Prophet ﷺ was told regarding the hypocrites: “Whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them—if you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times—Allah will not forgive them” (9:80). Put this in perspective. This is the same Prophet ﷺ who didn’t even have to ask Allah ﷻ to change the qiblah—he simply looked at the sky, and Allah ﷻ changed the prayer direction for the entire earth. Yet now, he’s not just looking—he’s asking, up to seventy times, “Forgive these people,” and Allah ﷻ says no. He will not acknowledge this request.

How intensely displeased must Allah ﷻ be that He refuses the intercession of His beloved Messenger ﷺ, made repeatedly, for these individuals? This becomes even more profound when we remember that it is the shafa’ah (intercession) of Rasulullah ﷺ on the Day of Judgment that will qualify countless people in this Ummah for Allah’s mercy. There will be people for whom the Messenger of Allah ﷺ will intercede, saying “They are with me”—and they will be granted mercy. But when you combine all these texts, you understand that the hypocrites will not be considered with him. They will not be counted among those who receive his intercession.

Surah Al-Hadid made this visually clear: a wall will be dropped between the believers and the hypocrites (57:13). The Prophet ﷺ won’t even see them on that Day. They won’t be counted among his followers. This matter is profoundly serious, more severe than we often recognize. The hypocrites took their oaths as a shield in this world, but on the Day of Judgment, no shield will protect them from the consequences of their deception.

Ayah 2–4 · linked video: 02. Al-Munafiqun (Ayah 2-4)

Surah Al-Munafiqun: Ayahs 2-4

Ayah 2

ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

They have taken their oaths as a shield, and turned others away from the way of Allah. How horrible is what they have been doing.

The hypocrites use their testimony of faith as a shield - اتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً . This reveals two types of hypocrites: the conscious spy who deliberately keeps his identity covert by claiming to be Muslim, and the subconscious hypocrite who doesn’t even realize he is continuously immersing himself in hypocrisy. The latter thinks to himself, “How can I be a hypocrite? I’ve said لا إله إلا الله محمد رسول الله. I’m a believer.”

Allah ﷻ concludes this ayah with إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - “how horrible the behavior, the actions they’re engaged in.” This phrase emphasizes that it is their behavior, their actions, that landed them in this state. Actions have consequences. Our hearts are not separated from our deeds. What you watch, what you say, who you associate with, how you earn your money - these necessarily have spiritual impact. Damage your heart enough and you reach a state where it becomes irreparable. There is a point of no return in hypocrisy.

Consider the mercy of the Messenger ﷺ. When Allah ﷻ revealed that even if he asked forgiveness for the hypocrites seventy times it would not benefit them, the Prophet ﷺ said he would ask seventy-one times. He still cared about them deeply. This is the same man who was making supplication for people who caused him tremendous pain - people who accused his wife, the Mother of the Believers, of infidelity. Put this in human terms: if somebody accuses your wife of such a thing, how could you ever look at that person without rage? Yet not only was this about his wife, it was about the integrity of all of Islam, and still the Prophet ﷺ continued to pray for them. This love and mercy is unfathomable.

Yet as incredible as the love and mercy of our Messenger ﷺ is for the hypocrites who have reached the point of no return, Allah ﷻ says even his supplication is not enough. Allah’s mercy is greater than any creation’s, including our Prophet’s ﷺ. But Allah ﷻ closes the door on these people. They don’t deserve it. There must be some serious violation for this to occur.

There are two golden rules about hypocrisy that all of us should remember. First, nobody feels safe from hypocrisy except the hypocrite himself. If you or I start thinking we don’t have that problem, that is the ultimate proof that we do. Nobody is afraid of hypocrisy except a true believer. If I’m worried about hypocrisy, that’s still a sign it hasn’t invaded me yet, it hasn’t taken over. But if it ceases to worry me, then I know it’s there.

The second golden rule is that the signs of hypocrisy revealed in this surah and elsewhere in the Quran are measures to judge where I stand - not anybody else. Actions are related to the heart, but how they’re related is different for different people. Somebody arrives late to prayer - you don’t look at him and apply the ayah الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَن صَلَاتِهِمْ سَاهُونَ directly to him. Someone gets angry when criticized - you don’t immediately label him with the characteristics mentioned in the Quran. When you hear a khutbah about hypocrisy, you shouldn’t be sitting there thinking “I know exactly who needs to hear this. I wish they were here.” If you have someone’s picture in your mind, that’s the ultimate sign you have the problem. Nobody accuses anyone else of hypocrisy except if they are themselves infected with the disease. You and I are in no position to accuse anyone of being a hypocrite.

The hypocrites in the Medinan Quran were the ultimate enemies of the Prophet ﷺ, comparable to those who undermined the missions of Musa (AS) and Isa (AS) - the enemy from within. During the expedition of Tabuk, nine individuals who were spies - conscious hypocrites - attempted to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ in the dark of night. They wore black cloaks and attacked in a valley, but a few companions were able to ward them off. The attackers escaped and blended into the Muslim crowd. The next day, Allah ﷻ gave the Messenger ﷺ revelation containing the names of these nine individuals.

The Prophet ﷺ told only Hudhayfah (RA) these names, instructing him not to tell anyone else. When Umar (RA) came to Hudhayfah (RA) asking for the names so he could deal with them, Hudhayfah (RA) refused. Keep in mind, by this time Umar (RA) was already guaranteed Paradise. Yet he came and asked, “Is my name on the list?” Hudhayfah (RA) responded, “I’ve taken an oath not to say whose name is there, but I can tell you yours isn’t.”

Why share this story? Because if Umar (RA) cannot feel secure about his iman, then you and I certainly cannot. He didn’t even make a distinction between conscious spies and subconscious hypocrites with character problems. Obviously, subconscious hypocrites wouldn’t try to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ - only spies would. But in Umar’s (RA) understanding of Islam, when hypocrites are discussed, it doesn’t matter if they’re conscious or subconscious. He wanted to know if his name was there. If he couldn’t feel secure, then you and I can never feel secure about this subject. It is the scariest subject in Islam.

Ayah 3

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ فَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ

That is because they believed, then they disbelieved, so their hearts have been sealed and they do not understand.

Allah ﷻ explains how hypocrisy develops: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟ - “that is because they believed, then they disbelieved.” Think of a pendulum swinging back and forth. Or Newton’s cradle, where one ball strikes and sets the others in motion - eventually, that motion dies out. Consider a person who cannot swim: he comes up, goes down, comes up, goes down, comes up… and then stays down. There’s movement back and forth until it ends in life or death, because motion is life and stillness is death.

Iman is life, kufr is death. The hypocrite has days of iman, then days of absolutely no iman at all. Days of iman, then zero iman. This turbulent spiritual life goes up and down, up and down, until it eventually just dies. You’re not able to resuscitate it anymore. No jolt works. Nothing works anymore.

When the Companions (RA) realized this happens - that iman goes up and down, that you have good days and bad days - Abu Bakr (RA) heard this and came out of his house nearly delusional, saying “Nafaqa Abu Bakr, nafaqa Abu Bakr” - “Abu Bakr has become a hypocrite!” Umar (RA) caught him and asked what was wrong. Abu Bakr (RA) explained, “When I’m with the Prophet ﷺ, my iman goes up and I feel so strong. When I’m not with him, I don’t feel like that anymore. My iman goes down.” Umar (RA) said, “I’m like that too!”

Both were terrified that their iman fluctuated, so they went to the Prophet ﷺ and asked if they were hypocrites. The Prophet ﷺ consoled them, explaining, “No, if you had iman that stayed up all the time, angels would be at your beds saluting you and praying for you - you’d be non-human beings. This comes and goes. That’s not what I’m talking about here.”

There’s a difference between normal fluctuation and what this ayah describes. We all have good days in iman where during salah you’re crying, and other days where you can’t even remember how many rak’ahs you prayed. Those ups and downs are normal. But what this ayah addresses is more extreme: people who stop praying altogether for a while, saying “I’m just having a tough time right now, I’m not really into the deen thing. I’m about to pick it up again.” Then they take another vacation from religion, then pick it up again. That could be an avenue to hypocrisy - ءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا۟.

Another meaning of this phrase is that they came to Islam and realized it was asking too much. For all practical purposes, they weren’t going to give themselves to Islam, but they would hold onto the title. Practically, they’ve denied all of it. They’re not interested. The hardest part for the hypocrite was obeying the Prophet ﷺ. They didn’t obey him. That’s why they went out of their way to show people, “No, we like him. We believe he’s a messenger.” But following him? That’s fuzzy.

There’s a growing population of Muslims like this today - people who see Islam as a spiritual outlet, a way of connecting to God. Prayer exists, some rituals exist, the phrases “insha’Allah,” “masha’Allah,” “subhanAllah” are there. But their worldview and their definitions of right and wrong, their ethical standards, are not shaped by Islam at all. Ask them about various contemporary issues and you’ll find their thoughts are not shaped by Islam whatsoever. There’s some level of iman theoretically, but practically it’s a denial of these teachings.

What they want is simply to connect to God while living life their own way - essentially spiritual agnosticism. God exists, but not in anything practical in life, just in theory. “I believe in God, and religion is good, so long as it doesn’t interfere with my lifestyle.” It’s superficial, cultural Islam, social Islam. They care about Muslims - they do - but it’s not connected to the religion at all. It’s just about us as a people, like atheist Zionist Jews who care deeply about Jews but have nothing to do with Judaism. We’re reaching that stage in the Muslim ummah today.

I’m not telling you to hate such people. I’m telling you we have to recognize this reality because these people are going to be in your own family - cousins like this, uncles like this. We have to reach out to them. This won’t be addressed through televised debates. These are people within our own families.

The ayah continues: فَطُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ - “seals are placed upon their hearts.” Remember, there are two kinds of cleansing: spiritual and intellectual. Hypocrisy is a spiritual problem, so طُبِعَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ means their hearts are sealed - spiritual cleansing is no longer accessible. But you would think if the spiritual problem is permanent, at least they could still have hope for intellectual cleansing. Yet the next part says فَهُمْ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ - “they are not able to understand.” Understanding is an intellectual thing, not a spiritual one. But Allah ﷻ is telling you that once you’re spiritually closed off, your mind is shut from it too. It doesn’t matter how intellectually you argue about Islam with these people - it’s not going to reach them because the heart is sealed.

Ayah 4

وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسَامُهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَقُولُوا۟ تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ ۖ كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُّسَنَّدَةٌ ۖ يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ هُمُ ٱلْعَدُوُّ فَٱحْذَرْهُمْ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

And when you see them, their bodies impress you. And if they speak, you listen to their words. They are like propped-up planks of wood. They think every cry is against them. They are the enemy, so beware of them. May Allah destroy them! How are they deluded?

Allah ﷻ now describes the outward appearance of these hypocrites: وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسَامُهُمْ - “when you see them, their physical presence impresses you.” The word أَجْسَامُهُمْ specifically means their physical bodies. This word choice is powerful. Allah ﷻ didn’t say “their numbers impress you” - He said their physical bodies impress you, because the spiritual entity inside is dead. It’s just a physical body, material numbers. They have no spiritual weight. The Prophet ﷺ had followers around him, and it looked like there were believers in front of him, but Allah ﷻ calls them just أَجْسَامُهُمْ - hollow bodies.

وَإِن يَقُولُوا۟ تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ - “If they were to speak, you would listen to their words.” Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ that they try hard to gain his attention, and out of his courtesy, he gives them attention.

Then comes a striking comparison: كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُّسَنَّدَةٌ - “It is as though they are hollow barks, planks of wood that are leaning.” These are planks that look like they’re standing, but when you look closely, they’re actually leaning on something. They’re not standing on their own. The word مُّسَنَّدَةٌ means something that gains its strength by leaning on something else. In this sense, they’re leaning on the Muslim community, leaning on the testimony of faith to have their credibility.

This word also implies they’re hollow on the inside, like a tree eaten away by termites. You have the outer shell of a believer, but inside there’s nothing - no spiritual substance to it.

Then Allah ﷻ reveals another characteristic: يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ - “They assume every outcry, every criticism is directed at them.” They’re paranoid. They’re so concerned about keeping their reputation intact that whenever the Prophet ﷺ talks about the signs of a hypocrite or the weakness of iman, they think, “Why is he looking at me like that? Was he talking to me?”

Now understand: when you hear a khutbah about hypocrisy, when I hear a khutbah about hypocrisy, we should think it’s talking about us. But we shouldn’t think it’s a personal attack. There’s a difference. Every time advice or criticism is given, I should examine myself: Am I guilty of that? Am I responsible for that? But if I’m sitting there thinking, “Every time he said ‘munafiq’ he looked at me. Every time he mentioned something, he made eye contact with me. What’s his problem with me?” - that’s a guilty conscience speaking.

Some people have literally come up after lectures asking, “Why were you looking at me like that?” I respond, “Where were you sitting? I don’t even know you. I can’t even look down that far.” One person even asked, “Who paid you to give this talk about me?” This is the paranoia Allah ﷻ describes.

When you hear a reminder about the signs of hypocrisy, you should think about yourself, not about who else needs to hear this message. That’s what Allah ﷻ is teaching us - these ayahs are mirrors for self-examination, not weapons to judge others.

Finally, Allah ﷻ declares: هُمُ ٱلْعَدُوُّ فَٱحْذَرْهُمْ - “They are the enemy, so beware of them.” This is the ultimate warning. The hypocrites are identified as the true enemy - not an enemy, but THE enemy. They are more dangerous than the open disbelievers because they operate from within, undermining the community while appearing to be part of it.

The ayah concludes with قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ - “May Allah destroy them! How are they deluded?” This is a supplication against them, expressing how far they have strayed from truth despite having once been close to it.

Ayah 4–9 · linked video: 03. Al-Munafiqun (Ayah 4-9)

Surah Al-Munafiqun: Ayahs 4-9

Ayah 4

وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَهُمْ تُعْجِبُكَ أَجْسَامُهُمْ ۖ وَإِن يَقُولُوا۟ تَسْمَعْ لِقَوْلِهِمْ ۖ كَأَنَّهُمْ خُشُبٌ مُّسَنَّدَةٌ ۖ يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ هُمُ ٱلْعَدُوُّ فَٱحْذَرْهُمْ ۚ قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

What’s particularly ironic about this ayah is that it comes after the previous passages about not saying mean things to people and saying nice things instead. Yet here, Allah ﷻ describes the hypocrites in remarkably harsh terms.

The ayah continues with a striking psychological insight: يَحْسَبُونَ كُلَّ صَيْحَةٍ عَلَيْهِمْ (they assume every outcry is about them). Two people are talking, and the hypocrites immediately assume they’re being discussed. People engage in conversation, and they think it must be about them. What are they saying? What are they saying? This constant worry about what other people think is a profound sign of hypocrisy. What are people saying about me online? What are the comments under the video? This self-consciousness, this self-absorption – it’s a real problem.

Then comes the direct warning: هُمُ ٱلْعَدُوُّ فَٱحْذَرْهُمْ (They are the ultimate enemy, so watch out for them). Be careful of them. The phrase قَـٰتَلَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ carries multiple meanings. It can mean “may Allah ﷻ destroy them,” but because this form of the past tense can be used in Arabic for supplication, it also implies that Allah ﷻ has already destroyed them. In other words, the internal conflict they’re suffering from is already a form of punishment from Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ – where are they being headed? Which direction are they being pushed into? Into which falsehoods are they being thrown?

Ayah 5

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ يَسْتَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ لَوَّوْا۟ رُءُوسَهُمْ وَرَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ

When it’s said to them, “Come on, just come – the Messenger will ask forgiveness for you,” their response is telling. When Abdullah bin Ubayy, the leader of the hypocrites, walked away with his gang of 300 at Uhud, everyone knew who had left the battlefield. It wasn’t a secret. The behavior was obvious. But still, nobody pointed at him and called him a hypocrite. They didn’t do that.

The next Friday, when the ayahs were revealed about the incident with the hypocrites, he was in the front row. The masjid was crowded, and he was in the front row – the hardest row to leave. As the ayahs began coming down, he became so angry that he got up and walked out of the khutbah. People were walking in as he was walking out. “Get out of my way! I’m heading out. I don’t need to take this.” He took these ayahs about hypocrisy as a personal insult. Amazing.

Now these same people are being told: it’s okay, come back. What seems unforgivable can still be forgiven. Come on back. The Messenger ﷺ will ask forgiveness for you. Their reaction? لَوَّوْا۟ رُءُوسَهُمْ – they turned their heads. “He’s going to ask forgiveness for me? I need him asking for forgiveness? I need to apologize to him?” They turned their heads in arrogance.

وَرَأَيْتَهُمْ يَصُدُّونَ – you see them stopping. Someone tries to pull them: “Come on, come on, come on,” but they pull their hand back. يَصُدُّونَ means to stop oneself, but it also means to stop others. وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ – and they are deeply arrogant. This is particularly heavy because مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ indicates they are totally and permanently arrogant. They’re stuck in this state.

Ayah 6

سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَسْتَغْفَرْتَ لَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تَسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ لَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

It will be the same fate for them whether you ask Allah ﷻ to forgive them or you never ask forgiveness for them – it wouldn’t have made any difference. لَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ – Allah ﷻ is not going to forgive them. إِنَّ ٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ – without doubt, Allah ﷻ is not going to guide the corrupt nation.

Ayah 7

هُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ لَا تُنفِقُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَنْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ حَتَّىٰ يَنفَضُّوا۟ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ خَزَآئِنُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ

They’re the ones who say, “Don’t spend a dime on those people hanging around the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Continue to bankrupt them until they disperse and never come back.” This is talking about the Muhajiroun. Abdullah bin Ubayy said, “Look, these people have come from Makkah. These immigrants have no resources. They’ve taken our jobs. They’re holding our household incomes down. They’ve caused a problem, and these people are an economic disaster for us. The only way we’re going to get rid of this problem is to stop spending on them. Stop helping them in any way, shape or form until they disappear.”

Remember what we’ve already read about the Ansar: يُؤْثِرُونَ عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَوْ كَانَ بِهِمْ خَصَاصَةٌ – they give preference to others over themselves, even if they themselves are hungry. They’re starving, yet they’re giving to others. That was about the true believers. Remember the Ansar and how they treated the Muhajiroun? The hypocrites have the exact opposite attitude. Now they say, “Don’t spend a nickel on them. Don’t spend a dime. Nothing.”

This surah also relates to a fight that broke out among them. There was a dispute where, unfortunately, shaytaan got the best of some people and they started calling each other names. Tribalism emerged. Makkans versus Madinans. “What did you say about Makkans? What did you say about us?” Tension erupted among some Muslims, and the hypocrites took advantage. They jumped in and instigated it further. Abdullah bin Ubayy called the Muhajiroun “the dogs of Madina – stray dogs that got here. They live off of our leftovers.” Insults were building real aggression.

This teaches us something critical: it doesn’t matter how strong your iman is. Stereotypes, gang mentality, nationalism and tribalism can come back at any second. It’s part of our identity, and that can be a good thing, but it can turn lethal at any moment. You can’t get rid of it entirely. I’m always going to be Pakistani – I can’t just change that. My parents are Pakistani, it’s going to be there. I’m going to try my best not to be so nationalistic that I hate other nations or think less of them, because in some parts of Pakistan, you’re not really Pakistani until you insult Bangladeshis. You’re not really Punjabi until you make fun of Urdu-speaking people. Your sense of identity only gets validated once you put another group down. This is where racism and tribalism come from – these sick forms of nationalism.

At some level, loving your city is natural and embedded. The Prophet ﷺ loved Makkah – he said so openly. It’s not wrong to love the place you’re from or the people you’re from. The Ansar took pride in being Ansar. They wrote poetry about the Ansar, saying “we are awesome” – not at the individual level where someone walks around saying “I’m Ansar, I’m awesome,” but as a people, it’s fine to take pride in who you are. There’s nothing wrong with it. But watch it, because at any given moment, that healthy sentiment can turn into something lethal. It can become bias against others, aggression against others, very easily. It takes very little.

One of the ways these things get excited in people nowadays is through sports. Were Pakistan and India in the World Cup this year? The cricket final? When India won, all the Indian brothers in the masjid were distributing sweets and donuts – they won the World Cup. The Pakistanis were trying to be gracious: “That’s okay, it’s just sport.” But when it gets out of hand and people run into the stadium beating each other up, that’s when we need to take it easy.

Even the Sahaba (RA) weren’t free from this – it took work. In a culture where tribe was everything for thousands of years, where you’re nothing without your tribe, and your tribe is being insulted – “Yeah, I’ve been Muslim for a while, but come on, you can’t let people insult us like that.” It came back, and the Prophet ﷺ was extremely upset. He said, “Are you going back to Jahiliyyah after Islam?” It was very harsh.

Now the hypocrites claim: “Don’t spend on the Muslims, don’t spend on these Muhajiroun until they run back to Makkah.” But Allah ﷻ responds: وَلِلَّهِ خَزَآئِنُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ – Allah ﷻ owns the treasure vaults and treasures of the skies and the earth. You think you’re spending on them? You’re spending what He gave you. You’re not doing anybody a favor – you yourself are receiving the favor. وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَفْقَهُونَ – however, the hypocrites won’t understand.

Ayah 8

يَقُولُونَ لَئِن رَّجَعْنَآ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ ٱلْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا ٱلْأَذَلَّ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

As they’re coming back from an expedition, heading back toward Madina after the fight has occurred, they say: “When we head back to Madina, the most noble ones will expel from it the lowest ones.” By al-a’azz (the most noble), they mean the Madinan citizens. By al-adhall (the lowest), they mean the Muhajiroun. “We’ll expel them from our city. They were living off our scraps anyway. Now we should just get rid of them because they are a source of humiliation for us. Who do they think they are, stepping up to us like this?”

This attitude also came about because of how Allah ﷻ honored the Muhajiroun in Surah Al-Hashr. If you remember, the Muhajiroun were honored and then the Ansar were discussed. But the first group mentioned was the Muhajiroun – they were talked about first. The hypocrites were thinking: “The Muhajiroun are being praised first? They live off of us, and now they get a higher rank? The spoils of war are being given to them? What is this?”

Allah ﷻ responds: وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ – nobility and authority belong to Allah ﷻ alone. وَلِرَسُولِهِۦ – and to His Messenger ﷺ. وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ – and to the true believers. وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ – however, the hypocrites have no knowledge whatsoever.

Ayah 9

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُلْهِكُمْ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

At the end of this surah comes a remedy against hypocrisy, a direct command to the believers: “O you who have iman, don’t allow your money and your children to take you away from the remembrance of Allah ﷻ.”

What was the purpose of the command at the end of Surah Al-Jumu’ah? فَٱسْعَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ – hasten to the remembrance of Allah ﷻ. In that surah, two things could take people away from dhikr: business and entertainment. In this surah, what are they? Money and children. Money and children can take you away from the remembrance of Allah ﷻ. We’re being warned – these attacks come from multiple angles, and we have to watch out for all of them.

What does it mean that money and children shouldn’t take you from the remembrance of Allah ﷻ? It means remembering money, remembering the kids, thinking about money, thinking about the kids more than you think about Allah ﷻ. There’s no calculator you can use at the end of the day to measure: “Did the kids win today, or did Allah ﷻ win today?” This is an internal battle for all of us. How much are you thinking about your children? How much are you thinking about Allah ﷻ? How much are you thinking about money? How often do you log into your online banking to check your deposits and check your balance? How often do you remember Allah ﷻ? How often are you thinking about future plans – financial plans, business plans? How often are you thinking about Allah ﷻ?

It can be that you’re praying five times a day, doing ritual religious acts, but you’re not thinking about Allah ﷻ at all. It’s possible. It can become all artificial. All you’re really thinking about is money and kids. That’s all that’s on your mind all the time. And it’s so hard when you get older. It’s so hard once you start a family, especially when you’re the head of the family. Two things are on your head constantly: the bills, the savings, the expenses, the school, the homework, the graduation, the vacation this summer, the family trip, the Eid plans. Money and kids, all the time. It takes a really strong effort to keep Allah ﷻ in the picture.

Money and kids – you don’t have to make an effort to think about them because they’re in your face. You have to change the diaper, so your nose won’t let you forget the kids. But Allah ﷻ is not in front of our eyes. It takes a lot of work.

I remember a story from when I had only one daughter, many moons ago. One child – now I can’t even count how many. Six. Now I can’t even get their names right anymore. “Hey… I mean… child, don’t do that!” When you have one child though, you’re so obsessive-compulsive. It gets cured later. Everything about the first child is adorable. There’s an Arabic saying: القرد في عين أمه غزال – “The monkey in the eyes of his mother is a gazelle.” Everything about them seems adorable to you.

I was making salah at home one day. My daughter was crawling around, and out of nowhere, she put her hands on the ground, stood up, and said her first word: “Allah.” Allah ﷻ didn’t just give us vision to see the rectangle in front of us that we’re praying on. We have peripheral vision. On the corner of my eye, I saw my child standing up, saying “Allah,” and I was in that salah. The craziest thing – I was reciting Surah Al-Munafiqun, and the very next ayah I recited was this one: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُلْهِكُمْ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ – “O those of you who claim to have iman, don’t let your money or your children distract you from remembering Allah ﷻ.”

I was put in my place. SubhanAllah. I had forgotten that I even had a child in that moment, so overwhelmed by what I was seeing. وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ – whoever does that, then they are the ultimate losers.

This idea of family distracting you from the remembrance of Allah ﷻ will be mentioned in the next surah too, exploring how they can become a trial. But don’t let your money and kids distract you from remembering Allah ﷻ.

Some people misuse these ayahs and manipulate Muslims by making them think they have to join Islamic causes, and when they join those causes, they shouldn’t worry about their finances or their family. If they do worry, then those things are taking them away from the remembrance of Allah ﷻ, so they should be part of the cause. “So what if you’re not home 18 hours a day? Allah ﷻ will take care of them. You take care of His deen.” That’s manipulation. That’s not genuine. It’s not true.

We have to serve our deen, but part of our deen is taking care of our family – making sure they have a halal livelihood and that children are raised with a father present. That’s still part of the tarbiyah. It’s a false idea that somehow you’re going to serve the deen while the mother raises the children without the role of a father. How critical is the role of a father? How are you going to go away and do this and that, having no time for your family, and then stand in salah reciting how Prophet Ya’qub (AS) gave advice to Prophet Yusuf (AS)? Where are your boys, Ya’qub? Are you reciting this ayah but you’re not even a father? That’s not what the Quran requires.

In emergency situations – on the battlefield when Badr, Uhud and Ahzab came – yes, leave the family and join the army. It’s a draft, and if your son is old enough, he’ll join too. That’s a different situation. But under normal circumstances, we’re not supposed to abandon our family and say we’re serving deen. That’s not serving deen.

The biggest problem in the world today is the destruction of the family unit. It’s not political, it’s not ideological. The biggest problem is that parents don’t know how to raise children anymore. We’re being raised in a generation of corrupt human beings – humanity filled with people who weren’t raised properly. And we’re going to do da’wah and talk to people while not raising our own children? It doesn’t make any sense.

There are causes beyond our family – that’s absolutely true, and we will have to give time to them and make time for them. But it will not be at the expense of the rights of our family. However, if you’re so obsessed with your family, so consumed by them that you have no time for Allah ﷻ, no time for anything else, then there’s a problem. You were sent on this earth to serve Allah ﷻ too. Part of that is taking care of your family, but there are other parts as well, and we have to learn to find a balance.

Admittedly, it’s extremely hard to find that balance – to find time for your deen, for your own learning, for serving others, for helping others, for your family, for your career, for your advancement, for yourself, for your health, for your friends. There are so many things you have to balance and juggle in a week. If you can find a way to organize your week, you’ve organized your life.

Ayah 9–11 · linked video: 04. Al-Munafiqun (Ayah 9-11)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 9

Ayah 9

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُلْهِكُمْ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ

Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly here, warning them about two of the most powerful distractions in this life: wealth and children. The word تُلْهِكُمْ carries a profound meaning—it means to be so consumed by something that you forget everything else. It’s not just being busy; it’s being so absorbed that critical priorities slip away without you even realizing it.

Think about the challenge of balance in our lives. We need to give time to our deen, our families, our parents, our friends, ourselves. We need to exercise, learn, worship properly. There’s a lot on our plate. What often happens is we become good at some things and neglect others entirely. We focus on what we’re naturally good at and let everything else deteriorate. We don’t feel the consequences immediately, but eventually we discover we’re bankrupt in one area or another.

Many parents dedicate their entire lives to securing a bright future for their children. They enroll them in the best schools, save for their college education, plan for their weddings. After having children, they essentially stop living for themselves and live entirely for their kids. But here’s the tragedy: despite all this sacrifice, the one thing they fail to give their children is deen.

I’ve seen this pattern repeatedly. Parents who work nineteen-hour days, driving cabs, working at gas stations, saving every penny. Why? So their children can one day own a house, go to college, achieve what they define as success. But while they’re working those endless hours, they’re absent from their children’s lives. There’s no meaningful relationship, no real connection.

By the time these children turn eighteen or twenty, something tragic has happened. They’ve developed lives their parents know nothing about. They have deeper conversations with their non-Muslim friends than they ever have with their own parents. When they finally establish their careers, many simply abandon their parents because there was never a genuine relationship to begin with. This is how so many Muslim families have been destroyed—not by poverty or hardship, but by misplaced priorities.

Then parents come seeking advice: “Please, talk to my children about the rights of parents.” But what about the rights of children? Where were you when they needed you?

Surah At-Tawbah makes this even more striking when Allah ﷻ says He wants to torture the disbelievers through their wealth and children. The very things you worked toward your entire life become the means of your torture. Your life gets consumed from the inside, eaten away, and what remains?

This ayah concludes with a stark warning: وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ—“Whoever does this, then those are the true losers.” Real loss isn’t financial bankruptcy. Real loss is reaching the end of your life and discovering that everything you worked for had no lasting value, no benefit in the akhirah.

PRIMARY_AYAH: 10

Ayah 10

وَأَنفِقُوا۟ مِن مَّا رَزَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ فَيَقُولَ رَبِّ لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنِىٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ فَأَصَّدَّقَ وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

After warning about the distractions of wealth and children, Allah ﷻ now commands: spend from what We have provided you. Notice the connection to Surah Al-Jumu’ah, which establishes the methodology for the Prophet ﷺ. That surah offered practical means to implement that methodology. Now, Surah Al-Munafiqun presents us with a serious problem—hypocrisy—and points to one of its main solutions: spending in the way of Allah ﷻ.

The phrase مِن مَّا رَزَقْنَـٰكُم is significant. Allah ﷻ doesn’t just say “spend your money.” He says “spend from what We have provided you.” If you look at the context of the previous surah, what is rizq? It includes your wealth, yes, but also your children, your time, your talents, your energy.

Some people are extremely religious themselves. They donate to masajid, perform Hajj regularly, attend every Islamic program. But when their own child says, “I want to learn more about Islam,” they respond, “No, you need to become a doctor. Study medicine. I’ll spend money on your education, but I won’t spend you for the deen. Not my child. Take a check instead, but don’t dedicate yourself to Islamic work.”

Allah ﷻ is teaching us to think of our families as a form of devotion to Him. It’s an opportunity for dedication. Husband and wife must remind each other that they are both devoted to Allah ﷻ. Together, they should remind their children that they exist to serve Allah ﷻ. They need to work toward that reality.

Have a career. Be a doctor, an architect, a businessman, an entrepreneur. Medicine isn’t evil. Business isn’t haram. But don’t forget why you’re here: to serve the deen. Allah ﷻ didn’t give us money so we could be “successful” by worldly standards. He gave us money so we could spend it for His sake.

Here’s something important to understand: you can’t spend until you first earn. The command to spend inherently requires financial success. You need to work toward that. But the question is: why are you working? So you can spend more for Allah’s sake. So you can give more.

The word أَنفِقُوا means to spend, but it encompasses everything—your youth, your talent, your creativity, your enthusiasm, your energy. All of it needs to be spent. The question every believer should ask is: How am I going to serve? How will my life mean something? How can I be of service to this deen?

We have career counselors in our communities, but we need deen counselors. Young people should be able to say, “I’m really good at math—what should I do for Islam? How can I use my skills to serve?” Because there are countless ways to serve the deen. You don’t have to be a scholar or a speaker. There’s so much work to be done, so many skills needed.

The ayah continues with urgency: مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ—before death comes to any one of you. What was the major problem mentioned earlier in this surah? The hypocrites running from death, thinking they could escape it.

Then comes the devastating scene: فَيَقُولَ رَبِّ لَوْلَآ أَخَّرْتَنِىٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ قَرِيبٍ—“Then he will say, ‘My Master, if only You would delay me for a brief term.’” Just a little while longer. A few more days. Just give me a nearby deadline, a short extension.

فَأَصَّدَّقَ—“Then I would give in charity.” The verb here is emphatic, showing determination: “I will definitely give charity, I promise!”

وَأَكُن مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ—“And I will be among the righteous.” This is the fantasy of the one facing death: if only I had more time, I would change everything. I would become righteous. I would give charity. I would fix my mistakes.

But there are no second chances. You’re not going to get to push a pause button and go back and collect a few more good deeds. When your deadline arrives, it arrives. The time to act is now, while you have breath in your lungs and strength in your body. Why worry about making excuses in the future when you can change your behavior today?

You’ve already been told this is coming. You’ve been warned. If you don’t get your act together now, why should you expect a second chance later?

PRIMARY_AYAH: 11

Ayah 11

وَلَن يُؤَخِّرَ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ closes this surah with finality: وَلَن يُؤَخِّرَ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهَا—“And Allah will never delay a soul when its time has come.”

The word لَن expresses emphatic negation. Never. Not ever. Not for anyone. When your appointed time arrives, there is no postponement, no extension, no negotiation. Unlike in this world, where you might beg an employer not to fire you and they might relent, or plead with someone for one more chance and receive it—with Allah ﷻ, when your ajal comes, it comes. Final.

The surah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ—“And Allah is All-Aware of what you do.” He has full knowledge, complete awareness of everything you’re doing right now. Every action, every intention, every moment of your life is known to Him.

The message is clear: get your act together now. Not tomorrow, not next week, not after you achieve this goal or that milestone. Now. While you still have time. While you can still make choices. While your deeds still matter.

May Allah ﷻ save us from being among those who lose. May He protect us from having a facade of good deeds only for them to be revealed as meaningless on the Day of Judgment. May He protect our iman and make it stronger with each passing day.
